core_behavior: The system must detect, classify, and respond to all cases where a user identity cannot be validated, authenticated, or authorized.

Rather than crashing, exposing internal errors, or silently failing, the system must return controlled, informative, and safe responses at every point where a user context is expected but cannot be satisfied.

Graceful handling is defined as: the system remains stable, provides a meaningful response to the caller or end-user, logs the event for operational visibility, and does not leak sensitive information.
inputs: Non-existent user: A user ID, username, or email that does not exist in the system.
Malformed identifier: A user ID or token that does not conform to the expected format (e.g., wrong UUID format, illegal characters, empty string, null value, excessively long string).
Expired credentials: A valid-format token or session that has passed its expiration timestamp.
Revoked credentials: A token or session that has been explicitly invalidated before expiry.
Tampered credentials: A token whose signature or integrity check fails (e.g., invalid JWT signature).
Reused single-use tokens: A one-time token (e.g., password reset, email verification) submitted more than once.
Suspended account: A user whose account has been administratively suspended.
Deleted/deactivated account: A user whose account has been soft- or hard-deleted.
Unverified account: A user who has not completed required verification steps (e.g., email confirmation).
Locked account: A user whose account is temporarily locked due to repeated failed attempts.
Insufficient permissions: A valid, authenticated user attempting an action they are not authorized to perform.
Cross-tenant access: A valid user attempting to access resources belonging to another tenant or user.
outputs: Non-existent user (authentication context): HTTP 401 Unauthorized, { "error": "authentication_failed", "message": "Invalid credentials." }
Malformed identifier / token: HTTP 400 Bad Request, { "error": "invalid_request", "message": "The provided identifier is malformed." }
Expired credentials: HTTP 401 Unauthorized, { "error": "token_expired", "message": "Your session has expired. Please log in again." }
Revoked credentials: HTTP 401 Unauthorized, { "error": "token_revoked", "message": "This session is no longer valid." }
Reused single-use token: HTTP 400 Bad Request, { "error": "token_already_used", "message": "This token has already been used." }
Suspended account: HTTP 403 Forbidden, { "error": "account_suspended", "message": "Your account has been suspended. Contact support." }
Unverified account: HTTP 403 Forbidden, { "error": "account_unverified", "message": "Please verify your account before continuing." }
failure_modes: Non-existent user: A user ID, username, or email that does not exist in the system.
Malformed identifier: A user ID or token that does not conform to the expected format.
Expired credentials: A valid-format token or session that has passed its expiration timestamp.
Revoked credentials: A token or session that has been explicitly invalidated before expiry.
Tampered credentials: A token whose signature or integrity check fails.
Reused single-use tokens: A one-time token submitted more than once.
Suspended account: A user whose account has been administratively suspended.
Deleted/deactivated account: A user whose account has been soft- or hard-deleted.
Unverified account: A user who has not completed required verification steps.
Locked account: A user whose account is temporarily locked due to repeated failed attempts.
Insufficient permissions: A valid, authenticated user attempting an action they are not authorized to perform.
Cross-tenant access: A valid user attempting to access resources belonging to another tenant or user.
edge_cases: Illegal characters in user ID or token.
Wrong UUID format for user ID.
Reused single-use token (e.g., password reset, email verification) submitted more than once.
Cross-tenant access: A valid user attempting to access resources belonging to another tenant or user.
security_constraints: The system must not crash or expose internal errors when a user identity cannot be validated, authenticated, or authorized.
The system must not silently fail when a user identity cannot be validated, authenticated, or authorized.
The system must not leak sensitive information in responses to invalid user scenarios.
The system must log invalid user events for operational visibility.
